pr_review_comment_body: No. Lambda functions are an exception to how pricing will be calculated for most other resources. Lambda function pricing is calculated based on the performance of all lambda functions. Other resources (such as api gateways, step functions, dynamoDB tables, etc) get their price calculated per resource. That data will be stored in the resource object and accessed through the graph, which is already passed.

I understand it's easier to call `otPlatCryptoDestroyKey()` here instead of in `KeyManager` (since it won't remember/save them). I think it's not the best model but It is fine and we can keep these calls here (I have been wanting to do an enhancement where we move all key and security related stuff that is now in `SubMac` into a module of its own, and we can address it when we add such enhancement)

Coupe of suggestions here:
- How about adding `#if OPENTHREAD_CONFIG_PLATFORM_KEY_REFERENCES_ENABLE` around these calls? 
   -  This way we don't need to rely on the platform behavior that `otPlatCryptoDestroyKey` should do nothing if not enabled
   - It is a safer model and platform implementation may choose to add an implementation `otPlatCryptoDestroyKey()` without it being enabled/used immediately (platform implementer may not realize that they need to have empty implementation thinking it won't be used unless PSA is enabled).

- I think we use `keyRef` value of zero as unused. We init them all as zero (calling `Clear()` on `mPrevKey`, `mCurKey` , `mNextkey` from constructor).
- I think we either need to check in the `SubMac::SetMacKey()` and skip calling `otPlatCryptoDestroyKey()` if the key ref is zero, or document that  we expect `otPlatCryptoDestroyKey()` would do nothing if the key ref passed to it is zero.
   - It is always better to be very explicit and explain in detail behaviors and expectations of the platform APIs. A platform developer may not look into the OT core code to see how the APIs are being used and just read and rely on the API docs only.

I referenced these posts [1](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/2881025/python-daylight-savings-time) (top answer only) and [2](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/39610892/in-python-how-to-check-if-a-given-time-is-in-daylight-saving-time-period). There are other suggested solutions online. I can summarize the problems with these solutions.

### Use of `localize`
[Source 1](https://gist.github.com/dpapathanasiou/09bd2885813038d7d3eb)
[Source 2](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/2881025/python-daylight-savings-time)
[Source 3](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/31146092/how-to-determine-if-a-timezone-specific-date-in-the-past-is-daylight-saving-or-n)

The root cause was inside `localize`. Using the `localize` to fix the problem with `localize` sounds counterintuitive. For instance, link 1 mentions the following in the final solution:

```
Once you've **localized** a datetime in fact you can simply check it's .dst() method to see if it's zero.
```

### `time.daylight`
[Source](https://www.geeksforgeeks.org/python-time-daylight-function/)

This solution might work, but the underlying code is just to do a similar check to what I do here. This code is cleaner, and I can change it if you would like.

### Calculations
[Source](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/38722792/check-if-date-is-in-daylight-savings-time-for-timezone-without-pytz)

One way is to calculate the timezone based on the current time and a previous time, but I think that this solution is long and too ad-hoc when we can achieve this with a few lines of code.

### Conclusion
I think that `time.daylight` or `time.localtime`+`tm_isdst` is the best way to go for this. If you believe in the other solutions, please let me know.

In my opinion, this is in line with style used for control/ser/measure commands that is largely used in the instrument code base (and, I guess, your concerns will also apply to them).

Linear and simple implementation is key IMO, but I prefer to offer implementers and users a quite natural way of expressing the instrument features, powerful enough to capture most cases and concentrate the extra complexity in a single place.

This will gently limit the programming style for channels definition on the instrument, allowing maintainers/implementers/users to enjoy the consistent style.

Of course, similarly to commands, when everything else fails, implementers can fall back in a custom style using the underlying API.

It's really a hardcoded constraint and the model cannot accept images that aren't divisible by 8 (even if it wanted to):

https://github.com/pytorch/vision/blob/74a1efcd06d9a72846f1805ce68b52cb5f01b2ea/torchvision/models/optical_flow/raft.py#L456

It has to be exactly divisible by 8 because we first downsample the inputs by 8, predict a downsampled flow, and then upsample the predicted flow by a factor of 8:

https://github.com/pytorch/vision/blob/74a1efcd06d9a72846f1805ce68b52cb5f01b2ea/torchvision/models/optical_flow/_utils.py#L26-L32

If the image wasn't a multiple of 8 to begin with, we wouldn't be able to upsample the flow to the right dimensions.


The fact that it's `8`  is somewhat arbitrary (we could downsample by 4 and upsample by 4) but we would still require images to be divisible by an integer N in general.

This makes me wonder: should we maybe remove the ``auto_now`` and ``auto_now_add`` on the model ?
https://github.com/ansible-community/ara/blob/7c5988ae7d0c1c1779b2437ff7c52e04e265c52d/ara/api/models.py#L256-L258

For example, if a host gets deleted, the latesthost reference will be updated and thus bumping the updated field but it doesn't mean the host that has taken it's place has been updated recently.

Sorting by created doesn't exactly work either because the latesthost will probably always have the same created date (unless it gets deleted and re-created).

If we remove the auto_now and auto_now_add, we would be "free" to put the dates we want in the sql migration as well as in the creation/deletion management:

But also, why abbreviate names like `self.header.field_records` → `frs`? It makes me have to go look them up. From the perspective of writing code, using long, fully qualified names feels redundant and you want to adopt a shorthand. But from the perspective of reading code, especially when hunting a bug or something where I'm only interested in a small local part of the codebase, answering questions like "what does "`frs`" mean?" is an extra burden.

I prefer fully qualified names, even if they're pretty long, to prioritize the needs of code-readers (over code-writers). I'm particularly aggravated when reading Python code that has a lot of `from some.library import SomeClass` at the top of the file, so when I encounter `SomeClass`, I have to go up to the top of the file to find out what it is and where it's defined. Maybe code analysis tools help with that, but those tools will get confused if there's too much/too complex indirection. I had to be won over to the idea of `import numpy as np`, but became okay with that when I saw how standard that particular substitution was. It's legal to `import numpy as qz`, but that would be terrible.

The downside of using fully qualified names almost everywhere is that code formatters limit the line length. I haven't entirely figured out how to reconcile the value of fully qualified names with how much black obfuscates a line when it's really long. So you don't have to use

```python
self.header.field_records[this_id]
```

everywhere you currently use

```python
fr
```

and get those deeply nested black-formattings. But maybe the substitutions don't have to be 2 and 3 letters long:

```python
field_records = self.header.field_records
field_record = field_records[this_id]
```

(and use them consistently in any function that uses it, kind of like how "`np`" always means "`numpy`")?

Yeah, I was considering if that would be the smarter way to do things. 

The `request` objects in the Django middleware stack are often mutated and attributes are added when processing the request, and I thought that doing a copy of the data adds overheads and might introduce strange bugs in some edge cases where the object state or reference to the request might actually be important, so I opted to go with adding the attributes and adding a signature to the places where the `request` is used.

This is less semantically valid, but might be more in line with existing practices of using the `request` as a store for all the related data.

I thought I'd go through the code bases of some competing implementations and established Django plugins with existing clean middleware solutions and see how they have done things for comparison.

I'm wondering if we should even support handling `np.ndarray` in this function.

Indeed, the data in a `np.ndarray` can have any format (for example, it can be a float array with range from 0-255), and we can't properly handle all possible cases. It's the responsibility of the user to do it. Plus, if the user passes OpenCV arrays to the function, it will be in `BGR` format (different from RGB from Pillow and what we use in torchvision)

As such, I think that we should probably only handle PIL Images -- handling numpy arrays is trivial from the user perspective (`torch.as_tensor(ndarray)`)

I see you've added it but honestly I don't think it should be a common process. It's difficult because an error may be treated differently based on what the caller is expecting. PowerShell has terminating errors and non-terminating errors and the behaviour of the latter can be terminating depending on the `$ErrorActionPreference` setting. The [HadErrors](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/api/system.management.automation.powershell.haderrors?view=pscore-6.2.0) is also a very weird property that I don't fully understand. It may be true depending on where the error/exception was raised but there are some cases where it has not but the caller may expect it to.

Personally I think the interface in `powershell.py` should try and match the actual .NET interface where possible to ease the transition for people who want to migrate their scripts from .NET to Python or vice versa. What you could do here is just create a common function in `client.py` to read the error's a PowerShell call and raise the exception. That way we have a common process abstracted out to a function but we keep the low level API more "pure".

I believe @bendichter is correct. Setting ``default=None`` I believe implies that None is a keyword argument with a default value of None. Since you didn't change how mode is being handled in the constructor, I think this means in this case that that self.mode in HDF5IO is being set to None and I think in turn h5py will end up choosing a mode, so effectively you still have an implicit default (i.e., whatever h5py does when you do h5py.File(..., mode=None)). I think this is also why the galleries are still passing tests, because there are quite a few instances in our tutorial galleries that do not set the mode, and all of those should fail tests if mode is a required argument. Note, when supplying no default arg in docval, then that means mode becomes a positional argument so one reason for the error you see could be that you inadvertently changed the order in which arguments are expected (which might explain the error you see).

> The proxy does not access Raiden's internal state, this can not be the reason.

Yes indeed. This is not the state I meant. And thinking about it context switching is not needed -- it's the waiting that worked.

What I meant is the onchain state by calling the `detail_channel()` function via the [_check_channel_state_for_settle](https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/blob/761bedfee2ee326401ad5ec95d55b1ab458a5213/raiden/network/proxies/token_network.py#L1151) call. 

- The 1 second delay allows for the block containing the partner's settle to be mined for the test.
- Then `detail_channel()` returns that the channel state is settled.
- And knowing this we can say that `estimateGas` failed since the channel is already settled.

But writing all these out I am sure that the 1 second of waiting there is not a good solution. It made the test pass but that's not guaranteeing that the race won't happen in a real scenario with longer block times. A different solution is needed.

The extra_data is used only by frames that need it.
The problem is that `software_device` uses `md_contant_parser` that saves the metadata as <enum, value> pairs, each the size of 12 bytes. That means that only 21 metadata items can fit in the 256 bytes `metadata_blob`. Current implementation was to throw all other metadata fields.
My implementation uses this vector to save all the fields that were previously discarded (and only them).

The drawback is that frames for other devices will need a bit more memory, but that seemed OK with all the temporary and other unused fields in the `additional_data` struct.

We considered several alternatives, and tested the performance of this implementation vs. keeping a database of metadata in the `dds_sensor_proxy`. This implementation was more efficient.

Additional considerations/changes that I'm putting here because of Github's UX:
- Removing commented-out lines. Not sure why we had those in the first place.
- According to https://github.com/mozilla/server-side-tls/issues/263, the relevant deprecation date is January 2020. So keeping `ECDHE-RSA-AES128-SHA` around shouldn't be relevant for the next release. My preference is to remove it, and update `SSLHonorCipherOrder` to `off`. If we do include `ECDHE-RSA-AES128-SHA`, `SSLHonorCipherOrder` should actually be `on`, as we'd prefer that clients don't use `ECDHE-RSA-AES128-SHA`, and therefore should honor server ciphersuite order. Whichever way we go, we should also fix NGINX's configuration, because we shouldn't both include `ECDHE-RSA-AES128-SHA` in the ciphersuites list and also let clients choose the order.
- Looks like `SSLOptions +StrictRequire` is still relevant for certbot reasons
- The mozilla recs don't mention `SSLCompression off` (which we have for non-centos systems) at all. The default is now `off` but it was `on` [before 2.4.3](https://httpd.apache.org/docs/current/mod/mod_ssl.html#sslcompression). We may want to suggest adding the `SSLCompression off` directive to the mozilla recs.

So, _technically_ (so long as you're using the public interface of a logger), wrapping a logger adapter with a logger adapter shouldn't actually break anything. You'd just end up with functionality from Both.

However, if you look at the way that this logger adapter is being used (and the _only_ way it's being used), it's being used on pretty much the first line of the `__init__` method of classes that don't have the logger injected. So in practice, the only way this scenario could happen would be if someone in their custom hook, resolver, or handler did something like: `self.log = MyOtherLoggerAdapter(self.log, self.stack.name)`. Even if they did, it wouldn't really break anything.

GitHub permissions are rather coarse. If a user has push permissions to a repo, that means they can delete branches.

The exception to this is branches with branch protection enabled. So, this will not delete, say, `development` when promotion PRs are merged.

The default behavior when creating a PR is that you give the repo owners permissions to push to the source branch on your fork. This means that any user with push permission on the target repo will be able to delete the source fork branch unless that branch has branch protections.

That's a great catch. This change actually hints at a general limitation we have in our approach (as you're well aware) - the way how we detect the resource state and the fact that we're allowing templates to be deployed on top of existing resources.

With the current implementation of our resource classes, in combination with the [change above](https://github.com/localstack/localstack/pull/7711/files#diff-95b30e7b954acaf70be05be62cb7404d8fef4ddf7538b924e12724f73f369bf0R1434-R1435), the `fetch_state` method can sometimes return false positives - i.e., report that the resource already exists when in fact it doesn't exist yet.

This issue in particular occurs if we have multiple instances of resource types whose deployment state cannot easily be determined based on the resource properties, e.g., consider the example below (we have a [similar example in our tests](https://github.com/localstack/localstack/blob/master/tests/integration/templates/template36.yaml#L15-L40)):
```
  routeTable1:
    Type: AWS::EC2::RouteTable
    Properties:
      VpcId: vpc-12345
  routeTable2:
    Type: AWS::EC2::RouteTable
    Properties:
      VpcId: vpc-12345
```

I've simplified the implementation - now the state lookup detects whether `PhysicalResourceId` has already been set (using the `result_handler` hook) and only then returns the resource details. This is quite a bit cleaner, and also should give us better parity with AWS (no additional tags). 👌

The fundamental problem here is that if we create multiple executors in the same thread (e.g., in the main thread), these executors will share the same temporary resources, which leads to race condition when these executors are used in different threads. To fix this problem, here we avoid creating executors when we create predictors in the main thread. The executors are actually created when the predictor is used in the worker thread for the first time. As long as the executor is always used in this worker thread, there won't be race condition.

Providing the update here for record-keeping and as punishment for myself for being so confident in my evaluation of the issue:

I was wrong! It looks like the issue with the matcher request wasn't actually making requests in quick succession, but rather making matcher requests in live mode with different recording ID scopes. In short, the matcher set request from `proxy_startup.py` applies to all tests, but the bodiless matcher requests made in these tests are specific to the test they're called from. The test proxy seems to be upset about that for reasons I don't yet fully understand.

The good news is that we were doing the wrong thing anyway, in a very-easy-to-fix way: we were sending matcher requests in recording mode when we don't actually use matchers in that case, regardless. This wasn't an issue in live test pipelines because recording is turned off in that environment, but when tests are being recorded locally we would hit the error mentioned above. Since we don't actually need these requests anyway, we can fix the issue by only sending matcher requests in playback mode (which is the only time matchers have any effect). And in playback mode, the whole recording ID scoping issue doesn't crop up.

Cat made the appropriate fix [here](https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-python/pull/23781/commits/6865341b0c076446fd73982cd4c3b091126014e5), so we should now be good to go 🙂

My strategy is:
- git clone is for development mode. Compilation toolset is required
- Users should install released versions from PyPI.
- Binary wheels are preferred, they don't execute `setup.py` code
- If a binary wheel for the target platform is not available source tarball is used regardless to `MULTIDICT_NO_EXTENSIONS`
- If the toolset is not available pure python installation is executed with a warning to stdout. Better to change stdout -> stderr as a minor improvement

Later I want to consider switching to pure python only if `MULTIDICT_NO_EXTENSIONS` is set to prevent silent performance lose. It enforces something like `MULTIDICT_NO_EXTENSIONS=1 pip install multidict` on machines without compilation toolset when a binary wheel is not available.

Blob sequences are always rank-1 string tensors, so:

  - the `ndenumerate`/`seq_index[0]` can just be `enumerate`/`idx`; and
  - if you like, you can avoid `make_ndarray`ing by directly
    dereferencing `string_val` on the `TensorProto` (unlike tensors with
    fixed-size dtypes, string tensors can only be represented with
    `string_val`, not `tensor_content`), which should save potentially a
    lot of memcpying, though the change is a wee bit sketchy due to
    poking into the proto internals;
  - you should probably validate this constraint (anything *transformed*
    by `dataclass_compat` is guaranteed to satisfy it, but users could
    write bad data directly into their logdir) before projecting down
    into a lower dimension.

@g-weatherill I guess you are using the `**kwargs` signature here because I said that it was okay to use it in the GMPETable case. Actually it was a lesser of two evils situation: in a situation when you do not know the parameters in advance and you cannot anticipated what in the future will go in the subclasses (as it happens for subclasses of GMPETable) using `**kwargs` is acceptable. If this is the situation here I will not protest. But if you know that the parameters can be at most a, b,c, sigma, etc, it is a  lot better to be explicit and to list them in the signature. The important thing is that the signature must be the same among all subclasses, i.e. it is okay to include parameters that are not used. Unless the number of parameters is huge or it is not known.

In short,
1. publisher holds only one subscriber
2. The only one subscriber holds a subscription from the publisher.

If the publisher and the subscriber only run at most one thread for each of them, this will be good.

Again, I think it's better not to attach a subscriber closely attached to the publisher. You can use the `subscription` object to send` promises` to the publisher's task queue (maybe multiple queues for each subscriber so that one copy of data can be sent to many). That can allow one publisher and multiple subscribers running in their own threads and do the test independently.

This should either be:

```
if !arg.signature.admits_type(&expected_arg) {
    ...
```

(That makes the implementing function arg _more_ permissive than the impl definition)

The problem with the check as it currently implemented can be seen with something like:


```
(define-trait trait-1 (
            (get-1 ((list 10 uint)) (response uint uint))))
```

Consider the following attempts to implement:
```
(define-public (get-1 (list 5 uint)) 
            (contract-call? contract get-1 u0))";
```
This should fail -- but I think would pass a check like `expected_arg.admits_type(&arg.signature)`.

```
(define-public (get-1 (list 15 uint)) 
            (contract-call? contract get-1 u0))";
```

This is actually okay --- since any caller can be sure that passing a `(list 10 uint)` would not result in a type error.

For return types, the directionality of admission is implemented correctly (the implementor's return type should be less permissive than the expected return type).

Fields that inherit from `String` don't have to store their deserialized value internally as a string. Because fields are both dumpers and loaders, it is better to think of `String` as `String -> Any -> String`. Subclassing `String` allows you to specialize `Any` to a more concrete type.

If there is no synchronization when finding free ports, I think there is a small chance that different tasks in the same machine will find the same free port. For example,

Task A in machine 1, runs into `find_free_port` and binds port 1234 and releases the port when return.
Then Task B in machine 1, which might be a little slower than Task A, could try to find a free port after Task A's `find_free_port` returns and finds 1234 again. 

This could result in different TensorFlow instances binding the same ip:port.

The text here should help the user make a decision about using the feature - including what the default behavior is, when it's good enough, and in what cases (likely rare) they might want to enable it. It's a feature we think most users should not use, so we want to communicate that too - and reassure that we're still comparing files on name/size and computing checksums prior to transmission.

After seeing the logic implemented , another idea would be to avoid the true/false option here (and a hidden default), and instead make the max size be a CLI option, e.g.

```
--compare-checksum-size-limit: Default 10MB. Prior to upload, changes to local files are 
detected by comparing file name and size. If both name and size match, and the file is below
 this limit, checksums are computed as a last resort. If file is above this limit, modifications 
that do not change the size of the file will not be detected for uploads.

Checksums take more time to compute on larger files, but the size may be more likely to 
change. If file modifications not detected for upload, consider raising this limit.
```

That way we can highlight why we chose what we did and give a clear path to change it if necessary.
We'd still have to have a way to specify __no limit__, but I'll put this out for debate first 😄

I've been thinking a bit more about this and I believe for the time being we should leave it at the count based progress.

The main reason being files that can be rather huge but processed very quickly, especially generated and duplicate files. For a decent progress, there would probably need to be two passes:
1. Detect and process files that do not have to be lexed by pygments
2. Process files that are lexed by pygments.

For a total progress, these two passes would need to be accounted for by some heuristic, like pass 1 = 10% and pass 2 = 90%. And the progress within pass 1 would be count based, within pass 2 size based.

But I'd rather we move this two a separate ticked I and look at it on my own (unless you really want to).

In another project I used this snippet:
```
def _is_parent_path(parent, child):
    """Return true if child is subdirectory of parent.
    Assumes both paths are absolute and don't contain symlinks.
    """
    parent = os.path.normpath(parent)
    child = os.path.normpath(child)

    prefix = os.path.commonprefix([parent, child])

    if prefix == parent:
        # Note: os.path.commonprefix operates on character basis, so
        # take extra care of situations like '/foo/ba' and '/foo/bar/baz'

        child_suffix = child[len(prefix):]
        child_suffix = child_suffix.lstrip(os.sep)

        if child == os.path.join(prefix, child_suffix):
            return True

    return False
```

I'm afraid you have that backwards. In `super(Class, self)` `__self_class__` is always the same as `type(self)`. It never changes and is the most derived class in the MRO (index 0).  It's `__thisclass__` that changes to reflect where in the MRO the new starting search point actually is.

```pycon
>>> class A(object):
...     pass
...
>>> class B(A):
...     pass
...
>>> class C(B):
...     pass
...
>>> class D(C):
...     pass
...
>>> D.__mro__
(<class '__main__.D'>,
 <class '__main__.C'>,
 <class '__main__.B'>,
 <class '__main__.A'>,
 <class 'object'>)
>>> d = D()
>>> sd = super(D, d)
>>> sc = super(C, d)
>>> sb = super(B, d)

>>> (D, sd.__self_class__, sc.__self_class__, sb.__self_class__)
(<class '__main__.D'>,
 <class '__main__.D'>,
 <class '__main__.D'>,
 <class '__main__.D'>)

>>> sd.__thisclass__
<class '__main__.D'>

这段代码是用来解析要求文件的函数，从这个补丁中，我注意到以下几点问题和改进建议：

1. 在修改 `reqs` 列表时，没有检查元素是否存在。可能会导致未找到元素而引发异常。可以在删除和添加操作之前使用条件判断来避免这种情况。

2. 代码中没有导入 `sys` 模块，可能会导致 `NameError` 异常。你需要在函数开头处加上 `import sys`。

3. 对于添加 `"dataclasses"` 的条件判断有一个误差。由于比较操作符的优先级问题，它首先会执行逻辑与操作，然后再与版本号进行比较。为了更清晰地表达意图，建议使用括号明确优先级，例如 `if sys.version_info.major == 3 and (sys.version_info.minor <= 6):`。

4. 考虑添加注释以提供更多文档说明。特别是可以解释每个操作的目的和实际作用。

综上所述，以下是对给出代码补丁的改进建议：

```python
import sys

def parse_requirements(filename):
    # 解析需求文件并返回需求列表
    with open(filename, "r") as file:
        reqs = [line.strip() for line in file]

    # 删除指定元素
    if 'facebook-wda>=1.3.3' in reqs:
        reqs.remove('facebook-wda>=1.3.3')
    if 'mss==6.1.0' in reqs:
        reqs.remove('mss==6.1.0')

    # 添加指定元素
    reqs.append('mss==4.0.3')

    # 如果 Python 版本 <= 3.6，则添加 dataclasses
    if sys.version_info.major == 3 and (sys.version_info.minor <= 6):
        reqs.append('dataclasses')

    return reqs
```

希望这些改进建议对你有所帮助！

> My understanding so far is that roughly:
`synchronous_mode: on`: sets `synchronous_standby_names`: `<synchronous_node_count> (<list_of_standbys>)`

Almost. It will be not a `<synchronous_node_count>`, but `len(<list_of_standbys>)`, where `<list_of_standbys>` are nodes that are eligible (state == 'running', don't have `nosync` tag, and don't lag more than `maximum_lag_on_syncnode`) to be synchronous and the total number of nodes in the list will not exceed `synchronous_node_count`.

> `synchronous_mode: quorum`: sets `synchronous_standby_names:` `ANY <synchronous_node_count> (<list_of_ALL_standbys>)`

Also almost.
- `<list_of_ALL_standbys>` -- not all nodes, but nodes with state == 'running', without `nosync` tag, and not lagging more than `maximum_lag_on_syncnode`.
- `synchronous_node_count` -- there will be a value `N`: `1 <= N <= min(synchronous_node_count, len(<list_of_standbys>))`

The main difference between `synchronous_mode` `on` and `quorum` that in the first case Patroni picks a fixed number of synchronous nodes and they are all synchronous. During failover with `synchronous_mode: on` we can **safely promote any node** from this  list and there will be no client visible data loss.

With `quorum` there will be something like `ANY 2 (n1, n2, n3)` in `synchronous_standby_names` and in order to guaranty that there is no data loss during failover Patroni has to check some minimum subset of nodes from this list to prove that the given node received not less WAL stream than others.
Example:
primary: n0
quorum: ANY 2 (n1, n2, n3)

That is, two nodes from this least could be promoted without client visible data loss, and every node need to figure out whether it has all data.
Lets say n0 and n1 are failed. But, we know that at least two nodes from n1, n2, n3 have the latest data, therefore at least one from n2, n3 also has it. Therefore n2 **must** see n3 and compare LSNs to make decision about promotion. If n2 is not behind of n3, than n2 could be promoted and there will be no client visible data loss.

@ro70 Sorry that I'm late to this discussion but DJA's *implementation* of filters assumes that they are ANDed together such that `?filter[tags]=1&filter[tags]=2` would never return a result as you are asking for the value of tags to be both 1 and 2 at the same time.  (This even reads as AND since the HTTP separator for query parameters is the `&` symbol.) I believe I said this pretty far back in comments to https://github.com/django-json-api/django-rest-framework-json-api/issues/718#issuecomment-541679826. This PR breaks that.

See the [documentation](https://github.com/django-json-api/django-rest-framework-json-api/blob/master/docs/usage.md#DjangoFilterBackend) of DjangoFilterBackend.

You can implement what you want with `?filter[tags.in]=1,2` by adding this to a view: 
```
class MyViewSet(ModelViewSet):
    filterset_fields =  = {
        'tags': ('in',)
    }
```

If you don't like having to expose the Django [field lookup](https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/3.0/ref/models/querysets/#field-lookups) nomenclature, 
you could probably also define a FilterSet named `tags` that implements `tags__in` something like this:

```
class TagFilterSet(filters.FilterSet):
    """
    Make a filter "alias" that implements `filter[tags]=1,2,3` without having to say `filter[tags.in]=1,2,3`
    """
    tags = filters.CharFilter(field_name="tags", lookup_expr="in")

class MyViewSet(ModelViewSet):
    filterset_class = TagFilterSet
```

So I don't think this PR is warranted at it is intentional not to allow repeated query parameters as they would confuse the client when they make the same error of assuming that repeats of the same query implies an OR rather than an AND. 

There is a mechanism I've outlined above (but not tested:-) that seems to get you the behavior you want.

@sliverc do you agree? We should close this PR and #718.

Perhaps a documentation PR is warranted to add how to configure a `filterset_class` [here](https://github.com/django-json-api/django-rest-framework-json-api/blob/master/docs/usage.md#configuring-filter-backends) -- something I also said in #718.

There is no `__hasattr__` hook in python, `hasattr` simply calls `getattr` and returns `False` if an `AttributeError` is thrown, and `True` otherwise. So there is no need to monkey patch it.

BTW, this also means that when running `if hasattr(obj, name) and getattr(obj, name) is not None` `obj.__getattr__` gets called twice, which could be avoided using a `try` `except` block, thought this might make the code read a little more clunky.

Once these classes are generated, you can customize them by dynamically adding or replacing attributes/function (a.k.a., monkey patching). 

A typical example is adding methods. Let's say you wanted a method that could
return data from only the first channel. You could add that method like this:

.. code-block:: python

    def data_from_first_chan(self):
        return self.data[:, 0]

    TetrodeSeries.data_from_first_chan = data_from_first_chan

You can also alter existing methods by overwriting them. Lets suppose you wanted to ensure that the
``trode_id`` field is never less than 0 for the ``TetrodeSeries`` constructor. You can do this by creating a new
``__init__`` function and assigning it to the class.

.. code-block:: python

    from hdmf import docval
    from hdmf.common.table import DynamicTableRegion

    @docval(*TetrodeSeries.__init__.__docval__['args'])
    def new_init(self, **kwargs):
        assert kwargs['trode_id'] >=0, f"`trode_id` must be greater than or equal to 0."
        TetrodeSeries.__init__(self, **kwargs)

    TetrodeSeries.__init__ = new_init

The above code creates a ``new_init`` method that runs a validation step and then calls the original ``__init__``.
Then the class ``__init__`` is overwritten by the new method. Here we also use ``docval``, which is described in the
next section.


.. tip::
    This approach is easy, but note your API will be locked to your specification. If you make changes to your
    specification there will be corresponding changes to the API, and this is likely to break existing code.
    Also, monkey patches can be very confusing to someone who is not aware of them. Differences 
    between the installed module and the actual behavior of the source code can lead to frustrated 
    developers. As such, this approach should be used with great care. In the
    next section we will show you how to create your own custom API that is more robust.

My understanding is that your concern is a general question in multi-threading (see the code below). In any multi-threaded system, we may expect this issue to exist -- and MXNet has multi-threaded engine, so this issue exists, right?

We have discussion that how about the content of array is changed somewhere inside the system but user don't know that, should we set up the `WRITEABLE` flag to be true to prevent users from writing, or should we completely disallow users to access this numpy array?

The question, in the high-level, is all about trade-off. Restricting the freedom of users may make things less error-prone, while giving users full freedom may make the system more powerful.

On one hand, in my humble option, restricting the freedom of users, like setting up `WRITEABLE` flag, does not completely resolve this issue, because users can still encounter pitfalls when they read the array using the numpy side API. The only helps in this case is to completely disable users from read or write the array, which grant them on access only the numpy side - This is more like guaranteeing safety by disallowing users to create threads/processes.

On the other hand, giving users freedom enables them to do more things, and does not necessarily mean that they will definitely make a mistake, because we will encourage to use mxnet's numpy compatibility, which is on the way.

Anyway, I am open to any discussion and open to possible changes setting up several flags on the numpy array.

Config mode sets these variables:

```
pybind11_FOUND - true if pybind11 and all required components found on the system
  pybind11_VERSION - pybind11 version in format Major.Minor.Release
  pybind11_INCLUDE_DIRS - Directories where pybind11 and python headers are located.
  pybind11_INCLUDE_DIR - Directory where pybind11 headers are located.
  pybind11_DEFINITIONS - Definitions necessary to use pybind11, namely USING_pybind11.
  pybind11_LIBRARIES - compile flags and python libraries (as needed) to link against.
  pybind11_LIBRARY - **empty.**
```

While this new implementation will work in happy path, the separation of `PublicClientApplication` and `ConfidentialClientApplication` in MSAL happens to make the current implementation fragile. For example, if a user (accidentally?) configure `username` and `password` AND `client_secret`, the current implementation would run into a `contextPub` undefined exception.

Let's do some refactoring.

```python
scopes = kwargs.get('scopes', ["https://datalake.azure.net/.default"])
if client_secret:
    app = msal.ConfidentialClientApplication(
        client_id=client_id, authority=authority+tenant_id, client_credential=client_secret, http_cache=_http_cache)
    out = contextClient.acquire_token_for_client(scopes=scopes)
    # for service principal, we store the secret in the credential object for use when refreshing.
    out.update({'secret': client_secret})
else:
    app = msal.PublicClientApplication(client_id=client_id, authority=authority+tenant_id, http_cache=_http_cache)
    if require_2fa or (username is None and password is None):
        flow = app.initiate_device_flow(scopes=scopes)
        print(flow['message'])
        out = app.acquire_token_by_device_flow(flow)
    elif username and password:
        out = app.acquire_token_by_username_password(username=username, password=password, scopes=scopes)
    else:
        raise ValueError("No authentication method found for credentials")
```

Hi @fenglu-g , I started working on the regex idea and I don't think it makes sense to have the user define the deduping logic. 

It seems more error prone to me to have the user define the job ID they want to use for deduping, since the job ID is not user defined, but is instead hardcoded by Airflow here as the task ID + first 8 characters of UUID: https://github.com/apache/incubator-airflow/blob/0e8394fd23d067b7e226c011bb1825ff734219c5/airflow/contrib/hooks/gcp_dataproc_hook.py#L94. Since Airflow sets the job ID, Airflow should also set the deduping logic.

I understand your concern about not wanting to hardcode the deduping logic, though. A solution for this could be: instead of having Airflow hardcode the job ID as in the link above, to have it instead call a function that creates the job ID from the task ID. And then my deduping logic would reference an inverse of that function to extract the task ID for deduping instead of hardcoding the deduping logic. 

In other words, Airflow would create the job ID with a function that maps the task ID -> job ID. And then the dedupe mechanism would call a function that maps from job ID back to task ID to use in deduping. Tests will be added to make sure that these two functions are inverses of each other.

So, it is the reason I initially stated in the commit message. Here is an excerpt:

> In order to fix it, `date_part` is wrapped into a `LiteralValue` to prevent
> quoting. To allow the `DatePart` enum to work also, it is derived from `str` as
> well as `Enum`, thus inheriting the required properties.

Here is a little more explanation:
Before Enum values were quoted properly, the `Extract` function was working fine, but only because `DatePart.year` for example wasn't quoted and stayed `YEAR` instead of `'YEAR'`. The same thing happened if you had called `Extract` with a string literal, instead of one of the Enum members (like `Extract("YEAR")`). Calling it like that produced invalid SQL but wasn't tested, instead relied on the old Enum behavior. To get around the issue, I wrap `date_part` in `LiteralValue` which only accepts strings, so it would fail with `DatePart.year`. Now strings worked, but the old behavior using the Enum doesn't. Here comes the `class DatePart(str, Enum)` making it work with enum members (old behavior) and strings (new behavior).

Filtering the _font_ metric values by type `italic angle` is necessary because just accessing `master.metrics[]` by index will not have a value for a _font_ metric that is an italic angle.

`update_or_create` uses `select_for_update` in the background if model exists. It's not supported to use `select_for_update` with nullable relations as mentioned in [Django docs](https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/2.2/ref/models/querysets/#select-for-update).

So, I could change the line to this:

```Python
access_token, _created = AccessToken.objects.select_related().update_or_create(...)
```

But calling `select_related` with no arguments only follows non nullable relations, nullable relations must be specified (see [docs](https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/2.1/ref/models/querysets/#select-related)). Since `user` and `application` are nullable relations, there is no point to call `select_related` with no arguments, and we can't specify this fields because of `update_or_create`.

No, it's not about the shims - we need those for backwards compatibility of config files (which I definitely approve of).  It's about retaining the old classes themselves, registered as `"old_*"`.  I don't think we need to do that, the classes should just be removed.

What's the worst case that happens to someone who has subclassed one of the classes we remove?  They copy the code from an earlier release of allennlp into their repo, change an import line or two, and everything still works.  I don't think that's a very bad outcome, and it saves us a significant maintenance and code complexity burden.  Backwards compatibility at all costs is far too extreme; this is a case where the costs are worth breaking compatibility, in my opinion, as the costs are low and the gain is high.

Two things here:
1) Better to accept a `Sequence` or `Iterable` instead of `List` in function parameters when it's possible to be more flexible. `Iterable` is when you only need to do something like `for x in xs`, whereas `Sequence` is when you need things in a consistent order and use functions like  `len()`. If it can be a `Tuple` or `List`, but nothing else, use `Sequence`. If it can also include things like `Set`, then use `Iterable`.
2. It's bad practice to set the default argument to `[]` because lists are mutable. See https://docs.quantifiedcode.com/python-anti-patterns/correctness/mutable_default_value_as_argument.html.  Instead, you can default to a tuple or change to `Optional[Sequence[Callable]] = None`.

Explaining the context for why code exists is important, especially maintaining code over the long term. I like to encode the decision and reasoning behind different actions in the code. 

```
/**
 * The performance tab shows performance-oriented information about a test run.
 * It helps users interact with the Firefox Profiler, and summarizes test
 * timing information.
 */
```

The component pre-amble explains why a component exists, what function it's serving, and why it's important.

```
// If there is a profiler link, show this first. This is most likely
// the primary action of the user here.
```

I used the primary blue here for the color, and want to let future readers know my intent for why this button is important. From a programming perspective, it doesn't what order components the buttons come in, but I would like to preserve the importance of keeping the button as a primary action. From a workflow perspective, putting something else first could regress it.

```
// Just to be safe, use the same isPerfTest check the other
// "Create Gecko Profile" button uses in the action menu.
```

For this check, I don't think it's completely necessary, but I want to be safe in case I present an action that the user can't perform.

I mean that I now have come full circle to embrace your original version where all the plotting methods are pulled out of `Atoms` and put into `visualization.Visualization`. However, unlike the current setup where the type of `Visualization` needs to implicitly be `Atoms`, I'm proposing that we go back to the old setup where it's just a regular class with a data field of type `Atoms`, then inside `Atoms` we instantiate it (`self.visuazation = Visualization(self)`, and wrap it's plot functions `def plot3d(self, *args, **kwargs): self.visualization.plot3d(*args, **kwargs)`.

I guess then we have a handful of dull lines where we wrap the plotting functions, but since we are only making quick aliases for the few public functions of `Visualize` it should be pretty short.

In fact, reading the further conversation it might be nice if `Visualize` had separate methods like `_plot3d_ase` and `_plot3d_plotly` (or whatever!), then a *single* public method `plot3d` which also takes the `method` kwarg. I found it a lot easier to read in the template file into a dictionary and do the diff on the dictionary itself rather than doing the diff on the templates. AWS's cfn-flip library lets you load a template, regardless of whether it is in json or yaml and whether it has `!Ref` or things like it in it. It loads it as a dictionary that can be compared key-for-key. This was really useful and tackles the resource and parameter ordering issue.

Take a look at #1093 and the output I posted there. I'm essentially doing this process:
1. Load the template to a dict with cfn_flip.load
2. diff the two with DeepDiff(deployed, generated, verbosity=2) (the verbosity of 2 shows exactly what the different values are so it's not just "item added" and "item removed"
3. I serialize the value to a readable json using `diff.to_json()` (which has some type serializatin functionality). If the sceptre output_format isn't "json", I convert the json to yaml.

This produces something like this:
```yaml
type_changes:
  root['Resources']['BucketPolicy']['Properties']['PolicyDocument']['Statement'][0]['Action']:
    new_type: list
    new_value:
      - s3:GetObject
      - s3:PutObject
    old_type: str
    old_value: s3:GetObject
```

The problem in current version of code is less about the frequency, but rather the assumption about condition checking is off. See this [line](https://sourcegraph.com/github.com/ray-project/ray/-/blob/python/ray/tune/trial_executor.py?L240). It incorrectly concludes if every time we arrive at this condition check with no running trial, we are not making progress - which is wrong.
E.g. [trial1(PENDING)] --> [trial1(TERMINATED), trial2(PENDING)] --> [trial1(TERMINATED), trial2(TERMINATED), trial3(PENDING)] etc. Every time, there is no running trial, but progress is made.

The current PR addresses this issue by changing the condition check to the length of all_trials, which corrects the bug.

As for the frequency, a warning message is printed for autoscaler case every 60s. For no autoscaler case, we will throw an error after 10s if the cluster doesn't have enough resources to fulfill a Trial, which is a hard fail(no repeated printing).

If 60s and 10s are too short, we can certainly elongate that. We just need to trade off with the time that user starts to wonder what is happening with pending trials without being informed.

(See some discussion here: https://google.github.io/styleguide/cppguide.html#Type_deduction)

My rule of thumb is that `auto` is okay if the type can be exactly deduced from immediate context, or if it saves at least one level of nested STL type nonsense. e.g. for
```
  std::map<std::string,std::vector<int>> m;
  for (const auto& kv : m) { // prefer `auto` over `std::map<std::string,std::vector<int>>::value_type` or `std::pair<std::string, std::vector<int>>`
```
but YMMV

```python
class MediaStorage(BaseStorage):

    def read(self, read_mode='rb'):
        if self.encoding is not None:

        with default_storage.open(self.get_full_path(), mode=read_mode) as f:
            data = f.read()
            if isinstance(data, bytes) and self.encoding is not None:

I changed that to `self` because having it as HttpLoggingPolicy caused a bug in ARMHttpLoggingPolicy.

In `ARMHttpLoggingPolicy`, we set the DEFAULT_HEADERS_WHITELIST to be `HttpLoggingPolicy`'s, plus a couple ARM specific headers (code [here](https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-python/blob/master/sdk/core/azure-mgmt-core/azure/mgmt/core/policies/__init__.py#L35)). However, by calling `HttpLoggingPolicy.DEFAULT_HEADERS_WHITELIST` here, we were setting the `allowed_header_names` of a `ARMHttpLoggingPolicy` to be just the `DEFAULT_HEADERS_WHITELIST` of `HttpLoggingPolicy`, not the `DEFAULT_HEADERS_WHITELIST` of `ARMHttpLoggingPolicy`. 

If you would like to still keep the code here as `HttpLoggingPolicy.DEFAULT_HEADERS_WHITELIST`, I can change the code in `ARMHttpLoggingPolicy` to override the setting of `self.allowed_header_names` to `ARMHttpLoggingPolicy.DEFAULT_HEADERS_WHITELIST`, I just went with this route because it was less code. That is a good question, one I hadn't considered. At least at first, a VirtualArray has no control over where the array will be created because it's created by a Python function (when using Awkward from Python; otherwise one has to override a callback, as in your .cpp tests).

I suppose we could add a `kernel::Lib` enum to the VirtualArray class itself and then as soon as a new array is created, it is copied. This immediate "copy to" would have to happen in `VirtualArray::array`, wrapping the output of `generate_and_check()`.

https://github.com/scikit-hep/awkward-1.0/blob/62d79bc3b8b2f38c472856e7b884f36b1eb95076/src/libawkward/array/VirtualArray.cpp#L272-L285

But it's the caching that makes this tricky. VirtualArrays call a function to produce an array but do not store the results—they would keep calling their function, re-producing their array every time if they don't have a cache to put it in. This cache is a MutableMapping on the Python side and has that kind of interface in C++, too. (The old version of this would attach the generated array permanently to the VirtualArray object, but that was a problem because users couldn't easily make them go out of scope. By putting this all in a cache that the user controls, users can manage memory by clearing that cache object, or using one with LRU eviction rules.)

The problem is that a cached copy of the array might be on the "wrong" device (main memory or GPU) for a VirtualArray of a given `cache_key` (string). We don't want to wrap the `cache_.get()->get(cache_key())` in the above function with a "copy to" because that would repeatedly copy it from main memory to the GPU (or vice versa) every time it is accessed. I think the right thing to do for VirtualArray is this:

   * If its `kernel::Lib` enum is cpu-kernels, then the only change in behavior is wrapping `generate_and_check()` with a "copy to main memory," which would be a pass-through if the generated array is already in main memory (which is usually the case).
   * If its `kernel::Lib` enum is something else, then we wrap `generate_and_check()` with a "copy to desired location," which might or might not be a pass-through, depending on whether the user was conscientious enough to write their generating function to agree with the location of the VirtualArray in which it is used (something that should not be assumed). The copy that we put in the cache is the copy in the desired location, and we use a "fully qualified cache key," the regular `cache_key()` suffixed by a colon and the name of the desired location, such as `"my-super-awesome-cache-key:cuda"`.
   * When retrieving an array from cache (and `kernel::Lib` is not cpu-kernels), we first check the fully qualified cache key, then check the regular cache key, and if both fail, generate again.

In summary, (1) cpu-kernels is special; it's the only library for which the fully qualified cache key is equal to the regular cache key. (2) The only copy of the array that gets cached is the one in the desired location, using a fully qualified cache key. (3) There are now two fallbacks when attempting to retrieve a key from cache, rather than one: first fully qualified cache key, then regular cache key, then regenerate. All of this is assuming that the cache exists, of course. (It's very simple when the cache does not exist!)

A smart user would keep a different cache for GPU arrays than main memory arrays because they're not interchangeable resources: freeing up main memory doesn't give you more GPU global memory, and vice-versa. We can't enforce that from here, but it would be one of the "best practices" that I'd talk about in tutorials. However, we can make the algorithm friendly to good practices: we don't want to put both the pre-copied and post-copied array into the same cache, since this could mix main memory and GPU in the same cache, or at least put two references to the same array in the cache (if it's already in the right place), which undermines the usefulness of an LRU algorithm. We do, however, want to fetch an array assuming that the cache might be mixed, because maybe the user isn't following best practices and re-copying an array from main memory to GPU is likely better than re-running the generator function (which usually comes from disk; much slower than the memory → GPU copy).

I'm concerned that introducing this `Reader` concept in addition to `Datasource` will make implementing custom datasources more difficult/confusing. The common case of subclassing `FileBasedDatasource` is covered since such subclasses can be `Reader`-agnostic, which is good, so this slight custom datasource UX degradation doesn't seem like a blocker.

It seems like the core reason that we can't do this read-time binding on the `Datasource` itself via its constructor (consolidating `Datasource` and `Reader`) is that the binding will need to be able to happen in a remote task, although we could work around this if we had `read_datasource` take an uninstantiated `Datasource`.

What do you think of this alternative, where the `Datasource` constructor is treated as the read-time binding? Note that this would be a breaking change for existing custom datasources.

```python
# datasource.py

class Datasource(Generic[T]):
    def estimate_inmemory_data_size(self) -> Optional[int]:
        return None

    def prepare_read(self, parallelism: int) -> List["ReadTask[T]"]:
        raise NotImplementedError

# file_based_datasource.py

class FileBasedDatasource(Datasource):
    def __init__(
        self,
        paths: Union[str, List[str]],
        filesystem: Optional["pyarrow.fs.FileSystem"] = None,
        schema: Optional[Union[type, "pyarrow.lib.Schema"]] = None,
        open_stream_args: Optional[Dict[str, Any]] = None,
        meta_provider: BaseFileMetadataProvider = DefaultFileMetadataProvider(),
        partition_filter: PathPartitionFilter = None,
        # TODO(ekl) deprecate this once read fusion is available.
        _block_udf: Optional[Callable[[Block], Block]] = None,
        **reader_args,
    ):
        _check_pyarrow_version()
        self._schema = schema
        self._open_stream_args = open_stream_args
        self._meta_provider = meta_provider
        self._partition_filter = partition_filter
        self._block_udf = _block_udf
        self._reader_args = reader_args
        paths, self._filesystem = _resolve_paths_and_filesystem(paths, filesystem)
        self._paths, self._file_sizes = meta_provider.expand_paths(
            paths, self._filesystem
        )

    def estimate_inmemory_data_size(self) -> Optional[int]:
        total_size = 0
        for sz in self._file_sizes:
            if sz is not None:
                total_size += sz
        return total_size

    def prepare_read(self, parallelism: int) -> List[ReadTask]:
        # ...

# read_api.py

@PublicAPI
def read_csv(
    paths: Union[str, List[str]],
    *,
    filesystem: Optional["pyarrow.fs.FileSystem"] = None,
    parallelism: int = 200,
    ray_remote_args: Dict[str, Any] = None,
    arrow_open_stream_args: Optional[Dict[str, Any]] = None,
    meta_provider: BaseFileMetadataProvider = DefaultFileMetadataProvider(),
    partition_filter: PathPartitionFilter = None,
    **arrow_csv_args,
) -> Dataset[ArrowRow]:
    return read_datasource(
        CSVDatasource,
        parallelism=parallelism,
        paths=paths,
        filesystem=filesystem,
        ray_remote_args=ray_remote_args,
        open_stream_args=arrow_open_stream_args,
        meta_provider=meta_provider,
        partition_filter=partition_filter,
        **arrow_csv_args,
    )

def read_datasource(
    datasource: Type[Datasource[T]],
    *,
    parallelism: int = -1,
    ray_remote_args: Dict[str, Any] = None,
    **read_args,
) -> Dataset[T]:
    # ...

    if force_local:
        read_tasks = _prepare_read(datasource, ctx, cur_pg, parallelism, read_args)
    else:
        # Prepare read in a remote task so that in Ray client mode, we aren't
        # attempting metadata resolution from the client machine.
        prepare_read = cached_remote_fn(
            _prepare_read, retry_exceptions=False, num_cpus=0
        )
        read_tasks = ray.get(
            prepare_read.remote(
                datasource,
                ctx,
                cur_pg,
                parallelism,
                read_args,
            )
        )

    # ...

def _prepare_read(
    ds_cls: Type[Datasource],
    ctx: DatasetContext,
    cur_pg: Optional[PlacementGroup],
    parallelism: int,
    kwargs: Dict[str, Any],
) -> List[ReadTask]:
    kwargs = _unwrap_arrow_serialization_workaround(kwargs)
    DatasetContext._set_current(ctx)
    ds = ds_cls(**kwargs)
    parallelism = _autodetect_parallelism(parallelism, cur_pg, ds)
    return ds.prepare_read(parallelism)
```

As for the `.log` that's slightly different. The purpose is to be a proxy for the other log. Theoretically, as a library, you don't know how logging is accomplished and you can't just find the logger because that's a lexical matter. So someone does a 
```
LOG = some_log_factory.make_some_log(__file__)
```
kind of call that sets up a log specific to this file and specific to its needs, and while you can make a logger that logs _somewhere_, it's not possible to guess the right place.

That's a separate question from whether I should have done:
```
def warn(*args, **kwargs):
    if self.log:
        self.log.warning(*args, **kwargs)
```
or
```
import logging
...
class LockoutManager:
    DEFAULT_LOGGER = logging
    def __init__(..., log=None):
        self.log = log or DEFAULT_LOGGER
```
The latter seems worth doing, I think. That would give the default effect of your `log_if_you_can` by making sure you always can, and then one could use `self.log.warning(...)` without conditionals.

I agree with you that if FTD switched to another partition with only `LeaderData` but without `RouteTLV`, the condition `mRouterTable.GetLeaderAge() < mNetworkIdTimeout` should be false since the router table will be empty until a `RouteTLV` is processed. In such case the assertion should not fail. 

However, consider such case:
* The FTD falls behind in `Router ID Sequence` because it failed to receive some `MLE Advertisements`
* A new Router joined the network, so FTD does not the Router ID in Router Table
* The new Router become Leader (the FTD still don't know)
* FTD receives an Data Response with new Leader ID, but its Router Table is still outdated. 
* FTD updates the Leader Data with new Leader ID

Note that now the FTD would have `mRouterTable.GetLeader() == nullptr` and `mRouterTable.GetLeaderAge() < mNetworkIdTimeout` both satisfied. 

With such case in mind, I think that we would have to recognize `mRouterTable.GetLeader() == nullptr` as a rare corner case which still could happen.

I was wondering what benefit we gain from not repeatedly converting the `trace_id` for multiple spans.

For a trace with 100 db spans, constructing the `traceparent` header from the `trace_id` and `span_id` took on my machine 24.5us.

```
root@b1754db4c2d8:/# python -m timeit --number=100000 --setup='from ddtrace.internal._rand import rand64bits; trace_id = rand64bits(); trace_id_hex = "{:032x}".format(trace_id)' '[("{:032x}-{:016x}".format(trace_id, rand64bits())) for _ in range(100)]'
100000 loops, best of 5: 24.5 usec per loop
```

If instead we already have the hex formatted `trace_id`, we get close to the same timing as we get if we do not include the `trace_id` in `traceparent`:

```
root@b1754db4c2d8:/# python -m timeit --number=100000 --setup='from ddtrace.internal._rand import rand64bits; trace_id = rand64bits(); trace_id_hex = "{:032x}".format(trace_id)' '[("-{:016x}".format(rand64bits())) for _ in range(100)]'
100000 loops, best of 5: 17 usec per loop
root@b1754db4c2d8:/# python -m timeit --number=100000 --setup='from ddtrace.internal._rand import rand64bits; trace_id = rand64bits(); trace_id_hex = "{:032x}".format(trace_id)' '[(trace_id_hex + "-{:016x}".format(rand64bits())) for _ in range(100)]'
100000 loops, best of 5: 18.7 usec per loop
```

@benwah this is an exotic example, but it demonstrates why the config check is still necessary:
```python
from pydantic import BaseModel
from typing import Annotated, Literal

from pydantic import Field


def to_upper(field_name: str) -> str:
    return field_name.upper()


class PyBase(BaseModel):
    class Config:
        pass


class Dog(PyBase):
    x: Literal["dog"] = Field("dog", alias='y')
    y: None = Field(None, alias='x')


class Cat(PyBase):
    x: Literal["cat"] = Field("cat", alias='y')
    y: None = Field(None, alias='x')


Pet = Annotated[Cat | Dog, Field(discriminator="x")]


class Model(PyBase):
    pet: Pet

m = Model.parse_obj({'pet': {'x': 'abc'}})  # note: due to aliases, x is NOT the discriminator
# On main:
"""
pydantic.error_wrappers.ValidationError: 1 validation error for Model
pet
  Discriminator 'x' is missing in value
"""

# On your branch:
"""
pydantic.error_wrappers.ValidationError: 1 validation error for Model
pet
  No match for discriminator 'x' and value 'abc' (allowed values: 'cat', 'dog')
"""
```
While the error is admittedly confusing either way (always a possibility when you have aliases that are actually names of other fields..), I would argue the error in this scenario is more correct on main than not.

Considering `model` is present in this function call I think it should be reasonable to check the value of the config setting and eliminate this (or other possible issues I haven't considered) as a potential regression.

If we allow `AssetsDefinition` (which definitely has a certain appeal) I think the usage of `Set` versus `Sequence` becomes increasingly problematic. I think it is more reflective in reality to say we take a sequence of things and then do runtime checks/processing to unique-ify. 

The previous `non_argument_deps` as typed the way it was for good reason, since strings and asset keys don't play together nicely in a set:

```
from dagster import AssetKey

string = "foo"
key = AssetKey("foo")
set([string, key])
# prints {AssetKey(['foo']), 'foo'} in interpreter
```

So if we want to mix and match these things it needs to be a `Sequence` IMO

@superwhd , I think the goal is to count the number of IP packets forwarded between infra link and thread (basically count packets being "border routed" between two networks).

I think the code here and the model of using `Ip6::HandleDatagram` and `aForwardThread` may not update the counter correctly and may over-count. 
- We can receive an IPv6 message over Thread radio which is passed to `HandleDatagram` and then we decide to forward it back into Thread (`aForwardThead` will be `true`).
- But then here we treat it as if it was from infra link and include in the counter.
- The most common example of this is when the device is the parent of some sleepy children, then other thread nodes will send the message destined for the sleepy child's address to the parent and then parent will forward it to sleepy child
    -  Parent of sleepy will respond to ddress query for all SED address and gives its own RLOC16. So other node will send their msg to the parent.

Yeah, I continue to think it's important to distinguish between "data" vs "deps". 

Thus far, everything defined in the `dependencies` field behaves fairly simply and consistently: we copy all relevant source files (possibly generated), and also add deps to all transitive deps. In the case of `python_distribution`, it does not have `sources`, so we simply add all its transitive deps.

I'm concerned about now having to keep track of all the different unwritten semantics. For example, this would be confusing imo:

- if you depend on most targets, you get the source code + deps
- if you depend on `pex_binary`, we'll build the PEX and insert at `<path>`. We won't include transitive deps.
- if you depend on `python_distribution`, we'll not only build it but also install it. We won't include transitive deps.

I fear it's often not apparent just by looking at target names in a BUILD file what is what:

```python
# What target is what? You have to either 
# use `./pants peek` or jump to BUILD files to find out.
python_tests(
   dependencies=[
      "src/py/project",
      "src/py/project:app",
      "src/py/project:lib",
],
```

Sure, people often use `pex-binary`, `lambda`, and `dist` to name things, but we don't enforce that nor even recommend it.

--

I acknowledge having a "special cased" field could be seen as clunky, but I think there's value in its explicitness:

- it's more clear when reading a BUILD file what will have magical handling. Strawman naming:

```python
python_tests(
   dependencies=["src/py/project:lib"],
   local_dists=["src/py/project"],
   runtime_package_dependencies=["src/py/project:app"],
)
```
- `./pants help` can explain how the special logic behaves—e.g. where `pex_binary` gets installed to. 

--

Special-cased fields also gives us much more flexibility: you can now use the same target type for multiple consumption purposes:

> what can a test do with a .whl file?

In your test, you can unzip the .whl and inspect that everything is there that you expect. In fact, Liam was working on a test to do this for PEP 561's `py.typed` file.

By having special-cased fields, you could use the same `python_distribution` to both a) install as a local dist you can import, b) inspect the `.whl` file, and c) use in some way we can't anticipate that plugin authors write. Or, pick and choose from those 3.

A really, really easy bug to introduce is to assign the `arch_prefix` later. The capture version is securely holding a reference to that variable, rather than capturing the current value, which is naively what most users would expect.

This makes the code much easier to read, since you don't have to inspect the contents of the function to notice it is actually capturing something in the outer scope. I'd maintain that any capture inside a function is harder to read than placing it into the signature - that's what the signature is for. I shouldn't be forced to read bodies of functions to know what variables are being used!

And yes, to the best of my knowledge, I always object to use of variable capture in reviews. (Actually, I object to nested functions in reviews, too, they reduce the readability and just tend to be used to capture variables, which is bad. I'm sure you can see such reviews in, for example, DecayLanguage & Particle where I've done exactly that. ;) )
